title: Sigmoid & Tanh: Squashing Functions
concept: Two classic smooth, S-shaped activation functions: Sigmoid squashes any number into a 0-to-1 range (like a probability), while Tanh squashes into a -1-to-1 range — both add the nonlinear bend a network needs.

exercises.prompt: Recap: what do activation functions add to a neural network that stacking straight layers alone cannot?

options.text: Nonlinearity — the ability to bend and curve

exercises.explain: Right — nonlinearity is the missing ingredient. Sigmoid and Tanh are two of the classic ways to add it.

cards.title: Squashing enormous numbers down to size
cards.body: A neuron's weighted sum can spit out a wild number — maybe 1,000,000, maybe -500. Feeding numbers that extreme straight to the next layer would be chaos. Sigmoid and Tanh are two functions built to squash any number, no matter how extreme, down into a small, tidy range.

cards.title: Sigmoid: squashing to 0–1
cards.body: Sigmoid takes any real number — positive, negative, tiny, or huge — and squashes it into a value between 0 and 1. That makes it feel like a probability, e.g. "0.8 confident this is a cat."

cards.title: Tanh: sigmoid's cousin that allows negatives
cards.body: Tanh works like a thermostat that can go from freezing cold (-1) through neutral (0) up to scorching hot (1), instead of only having an "off" and "on" setting. It squashes any number into a range from -1 to 1.

cards.title: The same input, two different squashes
cards.body: Feed a weighted sum of -2 into Sigmoid and it comes out near 0.12. Feed that same -2 into Tanh and it comes out near -0.96. Same input, different squash — because they cover different ranges.

cards.title: Not totally different tools
cards.body: You might think Sigmoid and Tanh are completely separate inventions. They're actually close cousins: Tanh is just a rescaled, shifted version of Sigmoid. Both produce the exact same smooth, S-shaped curve — just stretched to cover a different range.

cards.title: Sigmoid vs. Tanh ranges
cards.body: Same S-shaped curve, different output ranges:

exercises.prompt: What range does the Sigmoid activation function squash any input into?
exercises.explain: Correct! Sigmoid always outputs a value between 0 and 1, no matter how extreme the input.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Tanh squashes numbers into a range from -1 to 1.
exercises.explain: True! Tanh covers -1 to 1, unlike Sigmoid's 0 to 1 range.

exercises.sentence: Sigmoid and Tanh both squash numbers into a smooth, curved shape known as an S-shaped ___.
exercises.explain: Both functions trace the same smooth S-shaped curve — just over different output ranges.

exercises.prompt: A neuron's output needs to represent "how confident is the model this email is spam," as a value between 0 and 100%. Which squashing function directly fits that job?

options.text: Sigmoid, because its 0-to-1 range matches a confidence percentage
Tanh, because its -1-to-1 range matches a confidence percentage
Neither function can represent a confidence value

exercises.explain: Sigmoid's 0-to-1 range maps naturally onto a confidence percentage, which is why it's a common choice for that job.

exercises.prompt: You're building a neuron whose output should represent a confidence percentage between 0% and 100% for "is this photo blurry." Which squashing function is the natural fit, and why?

options.text: Sigmoid, because it squashes any input into a 0-to-1 range that matches a confidence percentage directly
Tanh, because its -1-to-1 range matches a confidence percentage directly
Neither — squashing functions can't represent percentages
Both are equally natural fits since they trace the same curve shape

exercises.explain: Exactly! Sigmoid's 0-to-1 output range lines up perfectly with a confidence percentage. Sigmoid and Tanh aren't the only options, though — up next is the activation function most modern networks reach for by default.
